analysis.note: Nominal predicate/head of the first clause. External sandhi: pariṇāmaḥ + ayam > pariṇāmo 'yam.
Proximal pronoun, “this,” agreeing with pariṇāmaḥ; initial a is represented by avagraha in sandhi.
Masculine nominative singular; construed with ellipsed pariṇāmaḥ, since upalabdhiḥ is feminine.
Genitive singular adjective qualifying viṣayasya: “of the sixfold object.”

verification.detail: compound stem ṣaḍvidha as Pum/Sasthi/Eka: pass; relation bahuvrihi: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Relative pronoun agreeing with upalabdhiḥ and correlated with sā.
Objective genitive dependent on upalabdhiḥ. External sandhi: viṣayasya + upalabdhiḥ > viṣayasyopalabdhiḥ.
Feminine nominative singular; here taken in the dictionary sense “apprehension/perception,” not merely “acquisition.”
Correlative pronoun referring back to yā ... upalabdhiḥ.

samasa.vigraha: kuśalā ca akuśalā ca advayā ca

analysis.note: Coordinate predicate compound agreeing with sā/upālabdhiḥ. The final member advaya is read privatively as a-dvaya, “neither of the two,” i.e. neither kuśala nor akuśala.

verification.detail: compound stem kuśalākuśalādvaya as Stri/Prathama/Eka: pass; relation dvandva: unsupported (logged)

justifications.decision: The first clause supplies the masculine head pariṇāmaḥ. Since upalabdhiḥ is feminine, tṛtīyaḥ cannot grammatically agree with it; the natural construal is “the third [transformation].”
options_considered: tṛtīyaḥ agrees with an ellipsed masculine pariṇāmaḥ
justifications.chosen: tṛtīyaḥ is masculine because it construes with ellipsed pariṇāmaḥ, not directly with upalabdhiḥ.

evidence.translation: “This is the second transformation; the third [transformation] is the apprehension ...”

justifications.decision: Both ṣaḍvidhasya and viṣayasya are masculine genitive singular, and their proximity supports adjective-head agreement: “of the sixfold object.” Reading ṣaḍvidhasya as dependent on tṛtīyaḥ would leave viṣayasya less naturally integrated with upalabdhiḥ.
options_considered: ṣaḍvidhasya is a partitive genitive governed by tṛtīyaḥ

evidence.translation: “the third: the apprehension of the sixfold object”

justifications.decision: The dictionary gives upalabdhi as “observation, perceiving, perception,” and viṣaya as “scope/range/object.” In this collocation, “apprehension of an object” is grammatically and contextually preferable to “acquisition of a territory/domain.”
options_considered: viṣayasya as possessive genitive belonging to the object
viṣayasya as objective genitive, the object apprehended
justifications.chosen: viṣayasya is an objective genitive with upalabdhiḥ, and upalabdhiḥ means “apprehension/perception.”

justifications.decision: The relative yā and correlative sā are both feminine nominative singular, matching upalabdhiḥ. Thus sā does not resume pariṇāmaḥ but the apprehension just named.
justifications.chosen: yā ... sā is a relative-correlative frame referring to upalabdhiḥ.

justifications.decision: The expression coordinates kuśala, akuśala, and advaya as nominative feminine predicates agreeing with sā/upālabdhiḥ. Since advaya follows the pair kuśala/akuśala, a privative reading “neither of the two” is preferable here to an abstract metaphysical “non-dual.”
options_considered: the final sequence as three loose sandhi-joined adjectives
the final sequence as a coordinate compound
justifications.chosen: kuśalākuśalādvayā is unpacked as “wholesome, unwholesome, or neither.”

translation:
This is the second transformation (pariṇāma).
The third is the apprehension (upalabdhi)
of the sixfold object (ṣaḍvidha viṣaya);
that is wholesome (kuśala), unwholesome (akuśala), or neither (advaya).
one_shot_delta: Without commentary, the exact referents of “sixfold object” are not fixed here; the grammar only supports “object of six kinds.”
A commentary-blind rendering could mistranslate upalabdhiḥ as “acquisition” rather than “apprehension/perception.”
A commentary-blind rendering could take advayā as doctrinal “non-dual” rather than the third alternative, “neither wholesome nor unwholesome.”
A commentary-blind rendering could miss the ellipsis in tṛtīyaḥ and try to make it agree with feminine upalabdhiḥ.
analyzer_disagreements: The api_seg output missegments the last pāda as maya_akuśala_advayā; the printed verse and local_S/local_SLM support kuśala-akuśala-advayā, so maya is rejected.

ByT5 exposes kuśala/akuśala/a/dvayā as components. I report the printed expression as the single inflected coordinate compound kuśalākuśalādvayā while preserving those components in the samāsa analysis.
open_questions: No commentary was supplied in this ablation, so the specific six objects meant by ṣaḍvidhasya viṣayasya cannot be established from the present evidence.
No commentary was supplied, so whether advayā is intended to correspond technically to avyākṛta/indeterminate remains unconfirmed.

The final expression may be represented either as a tight coordinate compound or as three sandhi-joined feminine predicates; I adopt the compound analysis because it matches the printed continuous form and the analyzer’s component analysis.
